basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: al-Muzzammil — “the Enwrapped” — opens with the tender call addressed to the Prophet wrapped in his cloak: “Rise to pray for a good part of the night.” It is the sūra of the night vigil, of the attentive chanting of the Quran, and of total surrender to God. Qushayrī unfolds here some of his loveliest pages: on the very title — O you for whom We made the night so that all people might rest in it, rise, you, that all may rest while you keep watch; on the “word of great weight” (qawl thaqīl) that descends, light on the tongue but heavy in the scale; on tabattul, total detachment turned toward Him; and above all on the verse “take Him as guardian” (wakīl), where he distinguishes the human agent — who spends from your wealth and demands a wage — from the divine Guardian: I provide for you from My wealth and give you the wage; your agent is beneath you and you disdain to speak to him — but I am your Lord, and I love that you speak to Me and I speak to you. The sūra ends on the easing of the obligatory vigil and the invitation to recite what is easy, to pray, to give, and to ask forgiveness.

versets.ar: يَا أَيُّهَا الْمُزَّمِّلُ
versets.tr: yā ayyuhā l-muzzammilu
versets.trad: O you who are wrapped in a garment!
versets.commentaire: al-muzzammil (المُزَّمِّل): the enwrapped, the one muffled in his garments. Qushayrī reports the scene: at the descent of this verse, the Prophet was wrapped in a cloak of hair. But he unfolds several spiritual readings: “O you wrapped in the burdens of prophethood... rise”; and the loveliest: “O you for whom We made the night so that all people might rest in it — rise, you, that all may rest while you stand.” The call is of an infinite delicacy: God does not jolt the sleeper, He names him tenderly by his very posture before calling him to the vigil.

mots.racine: ز م ل
mots.def: tazammala: to wrap oneself in one's garments.

versets.ar: قُمِ اللَّيْلَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا
versets.tr: qumi l-layla illā qalīlan
versets.trad: Rise to pray for a good part of the night:
versets.commentaire: qumi l-layla (قُمِ اللَّيْلَ): rise in the night, stand in prayer. At the heart of the enwrapping and the rest comes the call to standing. The night, which God made a garment and rest for all (s. 78, v. 10), becomes for the beloved the time of the vigil. “Save a little”: not the whole night, but a large part — for the way is not excess but just measure. The night vigil is the secret of the intimate: when the world sleeps, the heart stands alone before its Lord.

mots.racine: ق و م / ل ي ل
mots.def: qāma: to rise, stand; layl: night.

mots.racine: ق ل ل

versets.ar: نِّصْفَهُ أَوِ انقُصْ مِنْهُ قَلِيلًا
versets.tr: niṣfahu awi nquṣ minhu qalīlan
versets.trad: half, or a little less,
versets.commentaire: niṣfahu awi nquṣ minhu (نِصْفَهُ أَوِ انْقُصْ مِنْهُ): half of it, or a little less. Qushayrī specifies: the Prophet was left free, in the obligation of the vigil, between a third and a half of the night. The very precision is a gentleness: God imposes no rigid duration but a range, leaving love its margin. The vigil is no counted chore but a gift whose measure is settled between the servant and his Lord.

mots.racine: ن ص ف
ن ق ص / ق ل ل
mots.sens: or take a little from it

versets.ar: أَوْ زِدْ عَلَيْهِ وَرَتِّلِ الْقُرْآنَ تَرْتِيلًا
versets.tr: aw zid ʿalayhi wa-rattili l-qurʾāna tartīlan
versets.trad: or more, if you wish, and chant the Quran with care.
versets.commentaire: rattili l-qurʾāna tartīlan (رَتِّلِ القُرْآنَ تَرْتيلًا): chant the Quran with application. A pearl of Qushayrī: “graze your secret in its understanding, and take your time with your tongue in its recitation.” Tartīl is not haste but inhabited slowness: to articulate clearly, to let each word penetrate the heart. To recite is not to cover pages but to ruminate the meaning, to savour. The night is made for that reading: without witness, without haste, where the Word descends from without to within.

mots.racine: ز ي د
ر ت ل

versets.ar: إِنَّا سَنُلْقِي عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا
versets.tr: innā sa-nulqī ʿalayka qawlan thaqīlan
versets.trad: We are about to lay upon you words of great weight.
versets.commentaire: qawlan thaqīlan (قَوْلًا ثَقيلًا): a heavy word, of great weight. Qushayrī piles up the senses: “heavy, that is, light on the tongue but weighty in the scale”; “heavy, for it has weight and gravity.” He reports that when the Quran descended upon the Prophet mounted on his camel, the camel sank under the weight. And he adds: “heavy by its burden — except for the one upheld by a heavenly power and reared in the lap of nearness (taqrīb).” The divine Word has a real weight: the night vigil (v. 2) prepares the heart to receive it.

mots.racine: ل ق ي
mots.sens: We are about to lay upon you

mots.racine: ق و ل / ث ق ل

versets.ar: إِنَّ نَاشِئَةَ اللَّيْلِ هِيَ أَشَدُّ وَطْئًا وَأَقْوَمُ قِيلًا
versets.tr: inna nāshiʾata l-layli hiya ashaddu waṭʾan wa-aqwamu qīlan
versets.trad: Truly, the night prayer leaves a stronger imprint and its words are more upright.
versets.commentaire: nāshiʾat al-layl (ناشِئَة اللَّيْل): the hours born in the night, the night vigil. Qushayrī: “it is stronger in imprint (waṭʾ), that is, more in accord with the tongue and the heart, more vigorous”; and “more upright in word, for it is further from ostentation: the heart's presence (ḥuḍūr al-qalb) and the secret's stillness (sukūn al-sirr) are there fuller.” At night, far from eyes, prayer is truer: no one sees it, so nothing falsifies it. The night's silence attunes the tongue to the heart.

mots.racine: ن ش أ / ل ي ل
mots.def: nāshiʾa: what arises in the night, the watch hours.

mots.racine: و ط أ
ق و م / ق و ل

versets.ar: إِنَّ لَكَ فِي النَّهَارِ سَبْحًا طَوِيلًا
versets.tr: inna laka fī l-nahāri sabḥan ṭawīlan
versets.trad: You have, in the day, a long round of toil.
versets.commentaire: sabḥan ṭawīlan (سَبْحًا طَويلًا): a long going-about. Qushayrī: “sabḥ is going and speed — hence swimming (sibāḥa) in water; the meaning: your daytime occupations are many, and the night is freer for you.” The day is the time of action, of engagement in the world; the night, of recollection. Wisdom is not to flee daytime activity but to give it its night counterweight: to swim in affairs by day, to settle before God by night.

mots.racine: ن ه ر
س ب ح / ط و ل
mots.def: sabḥ: swift movement, occupation; ṭawīl: long.

versets.ar: وَاذْكُرِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ وَتَبَتَّلْ إِلَيْهِ تَبْتِيلًا
versets.tr: wa-dhkuri sma rabbika wa-tabattal ilayhi tabtīlan
versets.trad: Invoke the Name of your Lord and devote yourself wholly to Him.
versets.commentaire: tabattal ilayhi tabtīlan (تَبَتَّلْ إلَيْهِ تَبْتيلًا): detach yourself toward Him with a total detachment. Qushayrī: “cut yourself off toward Him with a complete cutting-off (inqiṭāʿan tāmman).” Tabattul is the cutting away of all that is not God, not by flight from the world but by the recentring of the heart. And it is joined to dhikr: to invoke the Name and to detach are a single movement — the more one remembers Him, the more one unbinds oneself from the rest. Detachment is not emptiness but fullness: one lets go of the many to cling to the One.

mots.racine: ذ ك ر / ر ب ب
mots.sens: invoke the Name of your Lord

mots.racine: ب ت ل
mots.def: tabattala: to devote oneself, cut off from all.

versets.ar: رَّبُّ الْمَشْرِقِ وَالْمَغْرِبِ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَاتَّخِذْهُ وَكِيلًا
versets.tr: rabbu l-mashriqi wa-l-maghribi lā ilāha illā huwa fa-ttakhidhhu wakīlan
versets.trad: Lord of the East and the West, there is no god but He. Take Him, then, as your guardian.
versets.commentaire: fa-ttakhidhhu wakīlan (فَاتَّخِذْهُ وَكيلًا): take Him as guardian, entrust your affairs to Him. Qushayrī opens here one of his tenderest pages: “If you take a creature as agent, it embezzles your wealth and demands a wage of you; if you take Me as Guardian, I make your wealth grow and give you the wage.” And further: “Your agent spends on you from your wealth; I provide for you and spend on you from My wealth.” And the summit: “Your agent is beneath you, and you disdain to speak to him much — but I am your Lord and your Master, and I love that you speak to Me and I speak to you.” To entrust one's affairs to God is no loss but infinite gain.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ش ر ق / غ ر ب
mots.sens: Lord of the East and the West

mots.racine: أ ل ه
أ خ ذ / و ك ل
mots.def: wakīl: guardian, trustee to whom one entrusts affairs.

versets.ar: وَاصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَاهْجُرْهُمْ هَجْرًا جَمِيلًا
versets.tr: wa-ṣbir ʿalā mā yaqūlūna wa-hjurhum hajran jamīlan
versets.trad: Bear patiently their words and withdraw from them with dignity.
versets.commentaire: hajran jamīlan (هَجْرًا جَميلًا): a beautiful separation. Qushayrī gives three admirable readings: “the beautiful separation is to associate with people by your outer self while distinguishing yourself from them by your secret and your heart”; “it is what is for your Lord's right, not for your ego's portion”; and the highest: “it is not to speak to them, but to speak to Me for them, by praying for them.” Beautiful distance is no hostile rupture but a withdrawal of the heart without contempt: present without, free within, and interceding in secret for those from whom one steps aside.

mots.racine: ص ب ر / ق و ل
ه ج ر / ج م ل
mots.sens: withdraw from them with a beautiful withdrawal

versets.ar: وَذَرْنِي وَالْمُكَذِّبِينَ أُولِي النَّعْمَةِ وَمَهِّلْهُمْ قَلِيلًا
versets.tr: wa-dharnī wa-l-mukadhdhibīna ulī l-naʿmati wa-mahhilhum qalīlan
versets.trad: Leave Me with the deniers who live in ease, and grant them a little respite.
versets.commentaire: dharnī wa-l-mukadhdhibīn (ذَرْني وَالمُكَذِّبين): leave Me with the deniers. Qushayrī: “do not trouble yourself over their affair, grant them a little respite, do not be concerned with them — I suffice you against them (anā akfīka amrahum).” A word of relief for the beloved: he need not carry the weight of retaliation. Ulū l-naʿma — the people of ease: often it is the affluent who deny, for abundance lulls. To them, a respite; to the Prophet, the peace of entrusting all to God, who takes charge of what is beyond him.

mots.racine: و ذ ر / ك ذ ب
mots.def: wadhara: to leave; mukadhdhib: who denies.

mots.racine: ن ع م
م ه ل

versets.ar: إِنَّ لَدَيْنَا أَنكَالًا وَجَحِيمًا
versets.tr: inna ladaynā ankālan wa-jaḥīman
versets.trad: With Us there are fetters and a blazing fire,
versets.commentaire: ankāl (أَنْكال): heavy fetters, irons. The respite granted to the affluent (v. 11) is no forgetting: with God are prepared the consequences of their denial. The chains speak of the confinement of the one bound by his refusal; the blaze, of the heat of what he has fed. As in the neighbouring sūras, the chastisement is not arbitrary but the ripened fruit of the choice: what one fetters within oneself ends by fettering us.

mots.def: ladā: with, in the presence of.

mots.racine: ن ك ل
ج ح م

versets.ar: وَطَعَامًا ذَا غُصَّةٍ وَعَذَابًا أَلِيمًا
versets.tr: wa-ṭaʿāman dhā ghuṣṣatin wa-ʿadhāban alīman
versets.trad: food that chokes, and a painful chastisement
versets.commentaire: ṭaʿāman dhā ghuṣṣa (طَعامًا ذا غُصَّة): food that sticks in the throat, that chokes. A cruel image: food, which should sustain, becomes what suffocates. A reversal of all gifts: the sweet water of creation (s. 77, v. 27) becomes boiling water, the food becomes a choking. The chastisement is the gift turned against oneself — for the one who refused to acknowledge the grace in the blessing receives its bitter reverse.

mots.racine: ط ع م / غ ص ص
mots.def: ghuṣṣa: what sticks in the throat.

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / أ ل م

versets.ar: يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ الْأَرْضُ وَالْجِبَالُ وَكَانَتِ الْجِبَالُ كَثِيبًا مَّهِيلًا
versets.tr: yawma tarjufu l-arḍu wa-l-jibālu wa-kānati l-jibālu kathīban mahīlan
versets.trad: on the Day when the earth and the mountains shall quake and the mountains become a heap of crumbling sand.
versets.commentaire: kathīban mahīlan (كَثيبًا مَهيلًا): a dune of sand that crumbles. The mountains — the very symbols of firmness (s. 78, v. 7) — become sand running through the fingers. On the Day, what seemed most solid comes undone. A striking image of the collapse of all false assurances: what man leaned on crumbles. Only what was founded in God remains stable; all the rest slides like a dune.

mots.racine: ر ج ف / أ ر ض / ج ب ل
mots.sens: the day the earth and mountains quake

mots.racine: ك ث ب / ه ي ل
mots.sens: the mountains become a crumbling dune

versets.ar: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا إِلَيْكُمْ رَسُولًا شَاهِدًا عَلَيْكُمْ كَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ رَسُولًا
versets.tr: innā arsalnā ilaykum rasūlan shāhidan ʿalaykum kamā arsalnā ilā firʿawna rasūlan
versets.trad: Truly, We have sent you a messenger who bears witness against you, as We sent a messenger to Pharaoh.
versets.commentaire: rasūlan shāhidan ʿalaykum (رَسولًا شاهِدًا عَلَيْكُمْ): a messenger, a witness against you. The Prophet is not only a warner but a witness: his presence attests that the call has been received. The parallel with Pharaoh (cf. s. 79) is a warning: a messenger was also sent to the tyrant, and his refusal destroyed him. History repeats: to each people its messenger, to each refusal its consequence. To receive a witness binds: one will no longer be able to say one did not know.

mots.racine: ر س ل
mots.sens: We have sent you a messenger

mots.racine: ش ه د
ر س ل
mots.sens: as We sent Pharaoh a messenger

versets.ar: فَعَصَىٰ فِرْعَوْنُ الرَّسُولَ فَأَخَذْنَاهُ أَخْذًا وَبِيلًا
versets.tr: fa-ʿaṣā firʿawnu l-rasūla fa-akhadhnāhu akhdhan wabīlan
versets.trad: Pharaoh disobeyed the messenger, and We seized him with a crushing grip.
versets.commentaire: akhdhan wabīlan (أَخْذًا وَبيلًا): a crushing seizure, heavy with consequence. Qushayrī glosses: “crushing.” Pharaoh disobeyed, and the sanction matched his excess. The example is set there for those who deny the Prophet: their fate could be Pharaoh's. But the verse also recalls (cf. s. 79) that the seizure came after long patience and a call full of gentleness. God seizes only at the end, when the refusal has definitively hardened.

mots.racine: ع ص ي / ر س ل
أ خ ذ / و ب ل
mots.sens: We seized him with a crushing grip
mots.def: akhadha: to seize; wabīl: heavy, dire.

versets.ar: فَكَيْفَ تَتَّقُونَ إِن كَفَرْتُمْ يَوْمًا يَجْعَلُ الْوِلْدَانَ شِيبًا
versets.tr: fa-kayfa tattaqūna in kafartum yawman yajʿalu l-wildāna shīban
versets.trad: If you disbelieve, how will you guard yourselves from a Day that turns children's hair white as the aged?
versets.commentaire: yajʿalu l-wildāna shīban (يَجْعَلُ الوِلْدانَ شيبًا): which turns children grey, white-haired. A striking image of dread: so terrible is the Day that even children would turn white. Qushayrī specifies: “it is by way of comparison” — to convey the intensity of the terror. If one has denied, how to protect oneself from such a Day? The question hangs unanswered: none can guard against it, save by present faith. The dread described is not to terrify but to awaken, while there is still time.

mots.racine: و ق ي
ك ف ر
ج ع ل / و ل د / ش ي ب
mots.sens: a Day that turns children grey

versets.ar: السَّمَاءُ مُنفَطِرٌ بِهِ كَانَ وَعْدُهُ مَفْعُولًا
versets.tr: al-samāʾu munfaṭirun bihi kāna waʿduhu mafʿūlan
versets.trad: Before such a wonder, the heaven shall split in dread, and God's promise will be fulfilled.
versets.commentaire: al-samāʾu munfaṭirun bihi (السَّماءُ مُنْفَطِرٌ بِهِ): the heaven splits by it (this Day). Qushayrī gives two readings: “split by the horror of this Day,” or “split by God, that is, by His command.” And the firm conclusion: kāna waʿduhu mafʿūlan — His promise is fulfilled. What God promises is as sure as if it were already done. The heaven itself, the vault that seemed eternal, yields before the fulfilment of the decree. Nothing resists the Word of the One who has promised.

mots.racine: س م و / ف ط ر
و ع د / ف ع ل

versets.ar: إِنَّ هَٰذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَن شَاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِ سَبِيلًا
versets.tr: inna hādhihi tadhkiratun fa-man shāʾa ttakhadha ilā rabbihi sabīlan
versets.trad: This is truly a reminder! Let whoever wills take a path toward his Lord!
versets.commentaire: tadhkira (تَذْكِرَة): a reminder. Qushayrī: “this sūra, or these verses, are an admonition; whoever takes its lesson is happy.” As in sūra al-Insān (s. 76, v. 29), the door is wide open: fa-man shāʾa — “let whoever wills take a path toward his Lord.” After the dread of the Day comes the free offer: none is forced, but the way is traced for whoever wills it. The reminder does not constrain, it awakens; and willing is enough to open the path.

mots.racine: ذ ك ر
ش ي أ
أ خ ذ / ر ب ب / س ب ل
mots.sens: take a path toward his Lord

versets.ar: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ تَقُومُ أَدْنَىٰ مِن ثُلُثَيِ اللَّيْلِ وَنِصْفَهُ وَثُلُثَهُ وَطَائِفَةٌ مِّنَ الَّذِينَ مَعَكَ وَاللَّهُ يُقَدِّرُ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهَارَ عَلِمَ أَن لَّن تُحْصُوهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ فَاقْرَءُوا مَا تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ عَلِمَ أَن سَيَكُونُ مِنكُم مَّرْضَىٰ وَآخَرُونَ يَضْرِبُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَبْتَغُونَ مِن فَضْلِ اللَّهِ وَآخَرُونَ يُقَاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ فَاقْرَءُوا مَا تَيَسَّرَ مِنْهُ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ وَأَقْرِضُوا اللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا وَمَا تُقَدِّمُوا لِأَنفُسِكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِندَ اللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْرًا وَأَعْظَمَ أَجْرًا وَاسْتَغْفِرُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ
versets.tr: inna rabbaka yaʿlamu annaka taqūmu adnā min thuluthayi l-layli wa-niṣfahu wa-thuluthahu wa-ṭāʾifatun mina lladhīna maʿaka wa-llāhu yuqaddiru l-layla wa-l-nahāra ʿalima an lan tuḥṣūhu fa-tāba ʿalaykum fa-qraʾū mā tayassara mina l-qurʾāni ʿalima an sa-yakūnu minkum marḍā wa-ākharūna yaḍribūna fī l-arḍi yabtaghūna min faḍli llāhi wa-ākharūna yuqātilūna fī sabīli llāhi fa-qraʾū mā tayassara minhu wa-aqīmū l-ṣalāta wa-ātū l-zakāta wa-aqriḍū llāha qarḍan ḥasanan wa-mā tuqaddimū li-anfusikum min khayrin tajidūhu ʿinda llāhi huwa khayran wa-aʿẓama ajran wa-staghfirū llāha inna llāha ghafūrun raḥīmun
versets.trad: Yes, your Lord knows that you, and a group of those with you, stand in prayer for nearly two-thirds, or half, or a third of the night. It is God who measures the night and the day. He knows that you cannot keep an exact count, so He turns to you with indulgence. Recite, then, of the Quran what is easy! He knows that among you there will be the sick, that others travel the earth seeking God's bounty, and that others fight in the way of God. Recite, then, of it what is easy, perform the prayer, give the alms, and lend God a goodly loan. Whatever good you send ahead for yourselves, you will find it with God, better and greater in reward. And ask God's forgiveness! God, in truth, is forgiving, merciful!
versets.commentaire: A long closing verse, where the rigour of the vigil (v. 2-4) is eased by mercy. ʿalima an lan tuḥṣūhu fa-tāba ʿalaykum (عَلِمَ أَنْ لَنْ تُحْصوهُ فَتابَ عَلَيْكُمْ): He knows you cannot keep an exact count, so He turns to you with indulgence. Qushayrī reports that some of the faithful, unable to measure the parts of the night, kept watch until dawn for fear of erring, until their feet swelled; God, out of mercy, lightened the burden. The lesson is immense: God knows human weakness and does not demand the impossible — “recite what is easy for you.” And the verse widens into a wisdom of life: the sick, the travellers, the fighters each have their measure. At its heart, the image of the qarḍ ḥasan, the “goodly loan”: what one gives to God is not lost but lent — one finds it with Him, better and greater. And the final seal: ask God's forgiveness (cf. s. 71, v. 10) — for even the most fervent vigil does not dispense from istighfār, the knocker at the doors of grace.

mots.racine: ع ل م / ق و م
mots.def: ʿalima: to know; qāma: to stand.

mots.racine: ق د ر
mots.sens: God measures the night and the day

mots.racine: ت و ب
mots.sens: He turned to you with indulgence
mots.def: tāba ʿalā: to turn toward, grant indulgence.

mots.racine: ق ر أ / ي س ر
ق ر ض / ح س ن
mots.def: qarḍ ḥasan: a goodly loan made to God.

mots.ar: تَجِدُوهُ عِندَ اللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْرًا وَأَعْظَمَ أَجْرًا
mots.tr: tajidūhu ʿinda llāhi huwa khayran wa-aʿẓama ajran
mots.racine: و ج د / خ ي ر / ع ظ م / أ ج ر
mots.sens: you will find it with God, better and greater
mots.def: khayr: better; aʿẓam ajran: greater in reward.

mots.racine: غ ف ر
غ ف ر / ر ح م
